title: Chapter 27: The Case
content: The flat smelled of toner and cold coffee.

Richard Mason sat on the floor of his Kensington bedsit, surrounded by paper. Not the elegant chaos of a designer's studio — this was lawyer's chaos, hierarchical and annotated. Bank statements fanned out to his left in chronological order. Corporate filings stacked to his right, flagged with colored tabs. In the center, the printouts Jane had smuggled out of Thornfield lay under a grid of handwritten notes, arrowed and cross-referenced, the connective tissue of a case being built from the inside out.

He rubbed his eyes. The laptop screen painted his face in pale blue, the only light in the room since he'd stopped opening the curtains three days ago. Outside, London carried on — sirens, buses, the indifferent hum of a city that had never cared about one woman locked in a basement in Paris. Inside, Richard was reading contracts the way Jane read garments: stitch by stitch, pulling at loose threads until the whole thing unraveled.

They were reading the same crime in two languages. She read objects — fabric, hardware, the physical evidence of a woman's existence reduced to a locked room. He read instruments — clauses, schedules, the legal architecture that had made that room possible. Between them, a picture was forming. Not a portrait. A schematic.
He reached for his coffee, found it cold, drank it anyway. The bitter dregs coated his tongue. Then he saw it.

The Seychelles entity — Enmerkar Holdings Ltd., the shell company that had been paying Dr. Fournier's consultancy fees — wasn't a single-purpose vehicle. It never was. Shell companies never are. They're vessels, built to hold whatever you need them to hold. And Enmerkar was holding plenty.

Richard traced the payments backward from Fournier's monthly retainer — €4,200, like clockwork, fourteen months running — and found a constellation. Other consultancy agreements, all routed through the same entity. A security consultancy firm in Lyon: €18,000 for "facility hardening and access control." The invoice was dated two weeks after Bertha's last public appearance. The locks on the basement door. A public relations firm in Mayfair: €6,000 monthly for "crisis communication and reputation management." That one had been running for three years — since before Bertha was confined. Anticipatory. A third payment, smaller, to an individual in Geneva whose title was listed as "personal welfare consultant." Richard hadn't identified that one yet, but the pattern told him enough.

Edward Rochester hadn't committed a crime of passion. He hadn't snapped, or lost control, or made a desperate choice in a dark moment. He had *budgeted* for this. The locks, the psychiatrist, the PR firewall — they were line items. Consultancy agreements. Renewables. The infrastructure of abuse, invoiced and filed, deductible against profits.

Richard sat back on his heels. The paper rustled under his shifting weight, and the sound was obscenely ordinary — the sound of documents being documents, when what they actually described was a man who had institutionalized cruelty the way other men institutionalize payroll.
He picked up his phone and called Jane.

She answered on the second ring, her face a pale oval in the screen. The white-noise machine hummed behind her — a steady, featureless hiss, like steam escaping from a pipe. She was in her room at the pension, door locked, curtains drawn much like his own.
"You've found something," she said. Not a question.

"The Seychelles company is a holding entity for a broader scheme." He held up one of the annotated pages to the camera. "Edward's been running a network of consultancy agreements through it. Fournier's just one node. There's the security firm that installed the basement locks. The PR company handling what he calls 'crisis management' — which, given the timeline, means silencing anyone who might ask questions about his wife. And at least one more payment I haven't identified yet. A person in Geneva."

Jane's eyes moved, processing. He'd come to recognize that look — the one where she was filing information alongside tactile memory, cross-referencing what he'd found with what she'd seen and touched. The bolts on the door. The sedation schedule Grace had described. The quiet, professional way an atrocity had been administered.
"It's a budget line," she said quietly.

"Exactly. He's not paying these people off after the fact. He *hired* them in advance. The PR firm was on retainer before Bertha was locked in. The security firm invoiced for the locks within days of her last public appearance. This is —"
"Institutionalized."
"I was going to say industrialized, but yes."
The white noise hissed between them. Richard could see Jane's jaw working, the muscle in her cheek tight. She was angry. Good. Anger was fuel. They'd need it.
"There's something else," he said. "And this is the part that worries me."

He reached for the Thornfield IPO prospectus — all 347 pages of it, obtained through a contact at the Financial Conduct Authority who owed him a favor and preferred not to think about what it was for. He flipped to the risk-assessment section and held the page to the camera.
"Read that."
Jane leaned forward. Her eyes tracked the text. Then stopped.
"'Material risks include reputational liability arising from prior employment disputes,'" she read aloud. Her voice was flat. "Employment disputes."

"He's framing it preemptively. Before any accusation is made, before any story breaks, the legal record describes Bertha's imprisonment as an employment dispute. When — *if* — this goes public, Thornfield's lawyers will point to this document and say the company disclosed the risk transparently. It's a hedge. He's building his defense before there's even a charge."

Jane's hand came up to her mouth. Not horror, Richard realized. Calculation. She was thinking three moves ahead, the way she did with fabric — seeing how a single seam determined the fall of the entire garment.
"The IPO," she said.
"Yes."
"Once Thornfield goes public—"

"The board closes ranks. Corporate protections activate. Legal privilege extends. Shareholder interests become the overriding concern, and any attempt to reopen what's been categorized as a resolved employment dispute will be framed as an attack on shareholder value. Bertha becomes a former contractor with a documented mental health history. Her story gets buried under quarterly earnings reports and non-disclosure agreements."
"And the truth?"

"Becomes a footnote in a legal filing that no one reads." Richard set the prospectus down. "Jane, the IPO is scheduled for the same day as the Paris show. The same day. You understand what that means. If Edward rings that opening bell — if Thornfield becomes a publicly traded entity — the legal architecture of the corporation will make prosecution exponentially harder. We have to strike before the IPO. Or during. Not after."
"The window is closing."
"Fast."

Jane was quiet for a moment. The white noise filled the silence like fog — dense, undifferentiated, the sound of a room trying very hard not to be overheard. When she spoke again, her voice had shifted. Not softer. More precise. The voice she used when she was measuring.
"Walk me through what we have."
Richard picked up a legal pad — his own handwritten summary, pages of it, the bones of a case.

"Financial records from the Seychelles entity, independently verified against public filings. That's the money trail. Video evidence from Grace — the surveillance feeds showing Bertha's confinement, the sedation schedule, the conditions. Blanche Ingram's testimony — she's prepared to give a sworn statement about what she witnessed. Grace Poole's cooperation — she'll testify to the day-to-day administration of the imprisonment, the frequency of sedation, the restriction of movement. My independent verification of the corporate structure. And your —" He hesitated. "Your physical investigation. The room itself, the objects, the evidence of sustained habitation under duress."
"Then we have enough."
"We have a strong case. Not an airtight one."
Jane's eyes narrowed. "What's missing?"
"Bertha."
The word fell between them like a stone into still water.

"Her direct testimony," Richard continued. "Without it, we have circumstantial evidence of confinement. The financial records prove Edward paid for the infrastructure. The video proves the infrastructure existed. But the *experience* of it — the duration, the intent, the psychological dimension — that only she can testify to. And she can't."
"Because she was declared mentally incompetent."

"Under French law, a person under tutelle cannot give sworn testimony. Her competency would need to be restored by a French court before she could take the stand. That requires a psychiatric evaluation — a new one, by an independent practitioner — and a judicial hearing. The fastest I've seen that process completed is four months. We have—" He checked the date on his laptop. "Eleven days."
Jane's face didn't change, but something behind her eyes did. A receding, like water pulling back from shore before a wave.
"Then we find another way."
"There isn't one. Not in time. The legal system doesn't accommodate urgency. It accommodates procedure."
"Procedure is built by people who had time. We don't."

Richard opened his mouth — to agree, to commiserate, to say something that would make the impossible deadline feel less like a wall — and then stopped. Because something Jane had said weeks ago surfaced in his memory, unbidden but perfectly clear.
"Recordings," he said.
Jane blinked. "What?"
"When you found Bertha. In the room. You said she had things — personal effects. A system. She'd been keeping track."
Jane's breath caught. He watched the realization move across her face like weather — first incomprehension, then understanding, then something close to awe.

"If she made her own documentation," Richard said, leaning forward, the laptop screen bleaching his features white-blue, "audio, video, anything that predates the diagnosis — it changes everything. It establishes her as a credible, self-aware witness who was actively gathering evidence *while* she was being declared incompetent. It reframes the entire narrative. She's not an unreliable narrator. She's a meticulous documentarian. She didn't just survive — she *recorded*. She was building a case of her own."
The white noise hissed. Somewhere in London, a siren wailed and faded. Jane's hand had dropped from her mouth to the desk, fingers splayed, as if bracing against the force of the idea.

"If those recordings exist," she said slowly, "they don't just support the case. They *are* testimony. Her testimony. In her own voice, on her own terms, made while she was still — while she still had—"
"Agency," Richard finished. "The thing Edward's been trying to prove she never had."

Jane looked at him through the screen. Her eyes were bright — not with tears, but with the fierce, focused clarity he'd come to associate with her best work. The look she got when she saw how a garment should be constructed, when the pattern resolved from chaos into intention.
"I need to go back to her," she said. "I need to ask her about the recordings. If they exist, if she kept them, where they are."
"Do it carefully. If Edward suspects—"
"I know."

She didn't say more, and he didn't ask her to. They both understood the geometry of the house on the Rue de Miromesnil — the angles of surveillance, the ever-present risk that Grace's cooperation could be discovered, the narrow margin between gathering evidence and becoming the next person locked away.

Richard gathered the pages on the floor, stacking them with the automatic precision of a man who had spent his career organizing other people's disasters into actionable sequences. The paper smelled of ink and warm toner, that chemical sharpness that always reminded him of new contracts — the smell of promises about to be broken.
He was about to end the call when something stopped him. A file he hadn't mentioned. A thread he'd pulled three days ago that had unraveled into something he still couldn't quite believe.
"Jane."
Something in his tone made her pause, hand halfway to the screen.

"There's one more thing." He pulled a manila folder from beneath the stack — thin, only four pages inside, but heavy in a way that had nothing to do with paper. "I looked into Edward's past. Before Thornfield. Before Bertha."
Jane went very still.

"There were two other designers. Both women. Both of whom left the company under non-disclosure agreements. One received a settlement — substantial, six figures, paid through the same Seychelles entity. She's alive. Living in Edinburgh. Not talking."
"And the other?"

Richard didn't answer immediately. He could feel the weight of what he was about to say, the way it would change the shape of everything that came after it. There were moments in a legal career — in any career that dealt in evidence — when information crossed a threshold from damaging to catastrophic. This was one of them.
"The other one died." His voice was level, clinical, the way he'd trained it to be when delivering facts that should not be true. "Suicide. Three years before Bertha was confined."
Jane said nothing. The white noise thrummed.
"The same psychiatrist signed off on her treatment before she left Thornfield. Fournier."

The silence that followed was not empty. It was full — gravid with implication, with the sickening logic of a system that had been running longer than any of them had realized. The consultancy network. The NDAs. The psychiatric evaluations. The PR retainer. Running before Bertha, through the unnamed woman, back to a beginning they hadn't known existed.

Richard watched Jane's face in the screen. Watched the arithmetic behind her eyes — not just the calculation of what this meant for the case, but the human accounting of it. A woman had died. A woman who had sat where Bertha sat, been seen by the same doctor, been processed through the same machine. And the machine had kept running. Had improved. Had budgeted for the next one.
Edward Rochester wasn't a man who had made a terrible mistake. He was a man who had built a system. And the system had a body count.
"We need a name," Jane said. Her voice was quiet and hard, like a blade being drawn slowly from its sheath.
"I'm working on it. The NDA is thick. The settlement — the one to the woman in Edinburgh — might be our way in. If she'll talk."
"Will she?"

"I don't know. But Edward's legal architecture was designed to make sure no one ever finds out about any of them. The NDAs. The psychiatric diagnoses. The consultancy payments. It's not just cover — it's *infrastructure*. He didn't hide what he did. He *systematized* it."

Jane closed her eyes. When she opened them, they were the eyes of a woman who had walked through a locked door in a basement and found another woman's life reduced to bolts and sedatives and a window that didn't open. A woman who understood, now, that the door she'd opened was just one of many.
"The IPO," she said.
"Yes."
"It's not just a financial event."
"No."
"It's a burial."

Richard looked at the papers around him — the evidence of a crime so thoroughly organized that it resembled, from the outside, nothing more threatening than a corporate flow chart. The contracts. The invoices. The risk assessments. All of it designed to inter the truth so deeply that no one would think to dig.
"Then we'd better make sure they don't get the shovels in the ground," he said.
Jane nodded once. The screen went dark.

Richard sat in the blue glow of his laptop, surrounded by paper, the smell of toner and cold coffee and something else — the sharp, metallic scent of a case that had just opened into a crater. Thirteen days. A dead woman without a name. A living woman who couldn't testify. And an IPO that would bury them both if he and Jane couldn't find a way to dig faster than Edward could build.
He pulled another folder toward him and opened it.
There was a woman in Edinburgh who wasn't talking.
It was time to ask her why.
